title: Quarter Kevu is keeping VCARB's title machine rolling
meta: Published: 17 March 2026 | By Tunde Adebayo, Feature & Human‑Interest Writer | F1CC News
caption: Quarter Kevu in the VCARB garage.
body:
He pulled off his right glove first, the way he always has, and let his fingers rest on the steering wheel for a moment longer than necessary. The engine was already silent, the Shanghai pit lane humming with the aftermath of another podium, but Quarter Kevu did not move. In the cockpit, the Estonian had the quiet stillness of a man who knows exactly what a second place in China meant — not a victory, but the unmistakable shape of a title defence taking form.

Quarter Kevu has spent the opening phase of 2026 carrying something heavier than expectation. The weight is not the championship itself — he has worn that crown before, in 2020, 2023, and again last year — but the standard that the VCARB garage has set across the last two seasons. The recent history behind the doors he walks through every race morning matters deeply: VCARB produced the 2024 drivers’ champion in Lukus Wright, a season of 264 points and relentless racecraft, then repeated the feat with Kevu in 2025, 259 points and a defence built on metronomic consistency. Two different drivers, two consecutive titles. That is not luck. That is a machine.

In Australia, the machine looked mortal for a moment. Kevu was quick enough to fight at the front of the midfield, his car showing pace in the wet that deserved more than the puncture‑scarred eighth place and a fastest lap that felt like a consolation. But he did not panic. He never does. “We know the rhythm of a long season,” he said quietly after Melbourne, the words carrying no frustration, just the calm of a three‑time champion who understands that the first race is not the last.

In China, the rhythm returned. Kevu qualified fourth, kept himself in the right window through a race that shredded strategies, and was perfectly placed when the Ferrari and Red Bull runners began to shuffle through the chaos. As Väisänen disappeared up the road on his hard‑tyre masterclass, Kevu did what he has always done: he stayed visible, he stayed smart, and he stayed on the podium. Second place behind the Finn meant 18 points, the kind of haul that quietly silences doubt. Across three rounds — Australia, the sprint, and the grand prix — he now sits on 27 points. That is exactly where a reigning champion should be: in the title conversation, rarely far from the front, and never wasting a weekend.

More importantly, VCARB are no longer a team merely trying to prove they belong at the front. Kevu has looked composed in traffic, sharp on restarts, and unusually effective at turning decent grid positions into proper points. There is no flamboyance, no desperate lunges, just the accumulation of laps and the patient extraction of everything the car can give. The team are still the reference point in their own garage — Wright, the other champion, watches from the other side of the engineering bay — and the rest of the field is still trying to catch them.

There is still a long season ahead, and midfield form tends to swing quickly once upgrades start to land. But if Kevu keeps pairing strong qualifying with clean race execution, if he keeps sitting in the cockpit for those extra thirty seconds after the engine dies, as if to remind himself that the work is never finished, then VCARB may spend far less time as a background team and far more time as the one everyone else is trying to catch. In the garage later that evening, a mechanic bent to wipe a smudge from the nosecone of the number 21 car — the same small gesture he had made after the title was secured in 2025. Kevu watched him for a moment, then turned back to the timing screen. The next race was already waiting.
